paper_title: Inhibitory effects of cinnamon oil, acetic acid, and lactic acid on Escherichia coli ATCC 23631

entities.description: The ability of substances to inhibit the growth or kill microorganisms like E. coli.
The process of treating and handling food to stop or slow down spoilage.
The interaction between cinnamon oil and organic acids that enhances antimicrobial efficacy.
Essential oil from Cinnamomum cassia used as a natural antimicrobial agent.
An organic acid tested for its inhibitory effects on bacteria.
An organic acid tested for its synergistic antimicrobial effects with cinnamon oil.
Assay used to determine the diameter of the inhibition zone.
Assay used to determine the minimum bactericidal concentration (MBC).
Method to evaluate cell membrane integrity by measuring leaked cytoplasmic contents.
Measured diameter of bacterial growth inhibition on agar plates.
Range of minimum bactericidal concentrations determined for the tested agents.
Minimum inhibitory concentration of cinnamon oil against E. coli.
Minimum bactericidal concentration of cinnamon oil in liquid medium.
The study of the physical, biological, and chemical makeup of food and the concepts underlying food processing.
Lead author of the study affiliated with Ho Chi Minh City University of Food Industry.
Co-author of the study affiliated with the Institute of Tropical Biology.
